segments.s013-g9: 'پژوهنده نامه باستان' reads as a noun phrase where 'پژوهنده' (an agent noun 'researcher/scholar') is linked to the thing studied 'نامه باستان' ('ancient book/record'). In classical Persian the ezāfe ('ِ') that would normally show the genitive relation is often omitted in verse, so interpret this as 'the scholar of the ancient record'.
segments.s013-g10: This is a relative clause introduced by 'که' ('who/that') describing the scholar. 'از' here marks partitive/genitive ('of/about'), so 'داستان از پهلوانان' = 'tales about/of the heroes'. 'زند' is an archaic/poetic third-person verb meaning 'recounts/relates' (Ferdowsi's usage); it functions as the finite verb of the relative clause. 'داستان' is the direct object 'story/tale'.
segments.s013-g11: 'چنین گفت' is a set phrase meaning 'thus it is said' or 'thus spoke/it is told'. The following noun phrase 'کآیین تخت و کلاه' is the content clause (object) introduced inline: 'کآیین' = 'that-rite' (classical fused form of 'که'+'آیین'), and 'تخت و کلاه' = 'throne and crown' (the paired regalia).
segments.s013-g12: Here 'کیومرث آورد' is a simple past clause: subject 'کیومرث' followed by the past verb 'آورد' ('brought/established'). The coordinating 'و' links the next clause 'او بود شاه', where 'بود' is the past copula 'was' and the predicate 'شاه' names his office.
segments.s013-g13: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; آمد is the simple past (3rd sg.) of آمدن 'to come' and precedes its complements in this poetic order. به introduces the goal/position 'into/to' (here: entering a zodiac sign), برج حمل is a genitival/attributive phrase where برج 'sign' is qualified by حمل 'the Ram/Aries', and آفتاب (the sun) is the logical subject that is understood as performing آمدن even though the verb comes earlier — a common VSO/verb‑front order in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s013-g14: جهان is the subject 'the world' and گشت is the past of گشتن used here in the copular/result sense 'became'. با introduces the comitative/ingenitive list of qualities the world acquired. فر is the technical, archaic term for royal glory or divine splendour; آیین names ritual/order/custom; آب literally 'water' (often with life/purity connotations). The sequence 'با فر و آیین و آب' enumerates what the world gained, attached to گشت.
segments.s013-g15: بتابید is the past (3rd sg.) of تابیدن 'to shine' used impersonally here ('it shone'). از آن سان is a fixed expression 'in that manner/so' or 'from that way' (آن + سان = 'that way'), and the poetic preposition ز (= از) introduces the source 'ز برج بره' = 'from the sign of the Ram/Aries'. Note the verb precedes the source phrase — a typical poetic word order where the sun itself is implied by the previous sentence.
segments.s013-g16: که here introduces a subordinate/result clause 'that/so that'. گیتی 'the world' is the subject and جوان گشت uses گشتن in the resultative sense 'became' with جوان 'young/renewed' as the predicate — 'the world was renewed/grew young'. از آن یک‌سره literally 'from that completely' functions adverbially to emphasize that the renewal was total ('entirely because of that').
segments.s013-g17: Subject + past copula: کیومرث (the subject) is followed by شد (past of شدن, 'became') and then the predicative phrase بر جهان کدخدای ('headman over the world'). بر here marks the domain/extent ('over/of the world') and کدخدای is the predicate noun 'headman'. The order is typical of Persian poetry where the predicate follows the verb.
segments.s013-g18: نخستین is an adverb/ordinal 'first' modifying the following clause. The prepositional cluster به کوه اندرون ('into/within the mountain') places location before the verb ساخت ('built/made'), and جای is the direct object 'place/dwelling'. ساخت is a simple past 'built'. This word order (locative before verb) is common in classical Persian verse.
segments.s013-g19: A coordinated subject 'سر بخت و تختش' ('the summit/top of fortune and his throne' = 'his good fortune and his throne') followed by the two-word verbal phrase بر آمد ('came up/rose'). بر here functions as a preverb 'up/on', joined to آمد 'came', and the destination به کوه 'to the mountain' follows. تختش contains the possessive suffix -ش 'his'.
segments.s013-g20: پلنگینه (leopard‑skin cloak) precedes the verb and is the object/garment named; پوشید is the past 'wore/put on'. خود is a reflexive/emphatic pronoun 'himself' (emphasizing that he dressed himself), and با گروه 'with a company/retinue' indicates accompaniment. Word order (object before verb, pronoun after verb) is typical of classical diction.
segments.s013-g21: از او 'from him' is the source phrase, اندر آمد is a compound past 'came forth/issued', and همی is an adverb meaning 'continually/always'. پرورش is the noun 'nourishment/upbringing/provision' — here meaning ongoing provision or maintenance. The whole segment says that continual provision proceeded from him.
segments.s013-g22: که introduces a result/explanatory clause ('so that/that'). پوشیدنی is a nominal meaning 'garments/clothing (to be worn)'; نو modifies it 'new'. The word بد in this poetic/classical context functions as an archaic contracted form of بود ('was') and should be read as the copula 'was' rather than the ordinary adjective 'bad'. سپس و نو خورش coordinates another predicate: 'and (his) food (خورش) new.' Overall: 'that the clothing was new and the food was new.'
segments.s013-g23: The locative phrase به گیتی درون ('in the world/within the world') comes first. سال سی is the classical order for 'thirty years' (noun then numeral), and شاه بود is the predicate 'he was king' (شاه = king, بود = was). Together this says he was king in the world for thirty years.
segments.s013-g24: به خوبی ('in/with goodness' — به used adverbially to form an abstract locative/adverb) qualifies his manner; چو 'like/as' introduces a simile. خورشید 'the sun' is the comparison, and بر گاه بود ('was upon the time') means 'was at its proper hour' or 'shone on its occasion' (بر = on/at, گاه = time/occasion, بود = was). The clause thus likens his goodness to the sun at its appointed time.
segments.s013-g33: This is an archaic poetic clause meaning 'a son was given to him'. پسر (noun) is the theme 'a son'. بد ... مر together form an old verbal idiom meaning 'was given/was born' (archaic usage not identical to Modern Persian بد). او را is the indirect object 'to him'. یکی خوب‌روی is an appositive nominal phrase 'a certain fair‑faced one' describing that son.
segments.s013-g34: هنرمند is an adjectival/nominal description 'skillful' or 'a man of art' attached to the son. و simply links clauses 'and'. همچون پدر is a simile 'like (his) father' and نامجوی is a compound adjective 'name‑seeking' (i.e., one that seeks a name/reputation), placed after پدر to qualify his similarity to the father.
segments.s013-g35: This clause states the boy's name and good fortune. سیامک is the personal name. بدش here is an older poetic construction meaning 'was given/was called' (i.e. 'was named'); ش is the possessive 'his'. نام = 'name' and فرخنده = 'auspicious/fortunate'. بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s013-g36: This clause means 'Keyumars's heart was alive for him' (i.e. Keyumars loved him). کیومرث را دل is a topicalized phrase: the proper name + object marker را marks Keyumars as the possessor/experiencer of the following دل ('heart'). بدو = 'toward him / for him'. زنده بود is literal 'was alive' used figuratively of the heart being 'alive' with affection.
segments.s013-g37: به جانش = 'for his life / to his very soul' (intensive object of emotion). بر از مهر = 'out of/for love' (بر ... از is a poetic adverbial phrasing meaning 'because of'). گریان is a participial/adjectival form 'weeping' and بدی here functions as an archaic past copula/auxiliary ('was' / 'became') producing 'he was weeping'.
segments.s013-g38: ز بیم = 'from/because of the fear'. جداییش = 'his separation' (جدا + ـیش 'his'); بریان is a figurative adjective 'consumed/burnt up' (by passion or grief). بدی again is the archaic past copula/auxiliary 'was', giving 'he was consumed'.
segments.s013-g39: بر آمد = 'arose / came up'. بر این کار = 'upon this affair' (بر + demonstrative + noun). یک روزگار is a temporal phrase 'one time / one era / one day' that locates the event; word order (verb before temporal phrase) is common in poetic narrative.
segments.s013-g40: فروزنده شد = 'became radiant/bright' (فروزنده is an adjectival/predicative form, شد = 'became'). دولت شهریار is a genitival/possessive noun group 'the king's state/fortune/sovereignty' (dولت 'prosperity/sovereignty' modified by شهریار 'king'), so the line says the king's rule grew glorious.
segments.s013-g1: 'سخن‌گوی دهقان چه گوید نخست' is an interrogative phrase: 'سخن‌گو(ی) دهقان' is an agent noun phrase ('the speaker/one who speaks, namely the peasant'), 'چه' is the interrogative 'what', and 'گوید' is the 3rd-person present (classical/narrative) form of گفتن used here to mean 'says' (a historic or general present). 'نخست' is an adverb 'first' specifying the order of what is said.
segments.s013-g2: This relative clause 'که نام بزرگی به گیتی که جست' modifies the preceding speaker: 'که' = 'who/that', 'نام بزرگی' = 'a great name' (here 'name' = fame, reputation), 'به گیتی' = 'in the world' (literally 'to/at the world'), and 'که جست' = 'who sought' where 'جست' is the past/poetic form of جستن meaning 'to seek'. The second 'که' heads the relative clause linking back to 'the one' (the speaker).
segments.s013-g3: The clause 'که بود آن‌که دیهیم بر سر نهاد' is a relative/interrogative formation: 'که بود آن‌که' = 'who was that one who...', with 'بود' the past copula 'was'. 'دیهیم' is a noun here (an old/local term, 'rural'/'village-related' or a title), and 'بر سر نهاد' uses the verb 'نهاد' (past of نهادن) with the prepositional phrase 'بر سر' meaning 'upon the head' — literally 'set/put upon the head'.
segments.s013-g4: 'ندارد کس آن روزگاران به یاد' is a negated existential-memory statement: 'ندارد' is present negative 'does not have' (here 'there is nobody who has/keeps'), 'کس' = 'anyone/no one' as the indefinite subject, 'آن روزگاران' = 'those ages/times', and 'به یاد' is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning 'in memory' or 'to (one's) remembrance' — altogether 'no one from those ages remembers it.'
segments.s013-g5: 'مگر' introduces an exception ('except that'); 'کز' (archaic contraction of 'که' + 'از') here means 'from'/'out of' and marks source ('from his father'), 'پدر' = 'father', 'یاد دارد' is a present construction 'has memory' = 'remembers/retains', and 'پسر' is the person possessing that memory ('the son'). Word order is subject (پسر) after the verb phrase, a normal option in poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s013-g6: 'بگوید تو را یک به یک در به در' continues the narration: 'بگوید' is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive used in narrative sense 'he will tell' or 'he tells', 'تو را' marks 'you' as the indirect/direct object (تو + object marker را), 'یک به یک' is the idiom 'one by one', and 'در به در' is an idiomatic reduplicated expression 'door to door' (literally 'door by door'), meaning 'in sequence, from place to place' — together: 'he will tell you one by one, from door to door.'
segments.s013-g7: This clause 'که نام بزرگی که آورد پیش' centers on a relative 'که' ('whose' / 'that'), 'نام بزرگی' = 'a great name/famous name' (name = reputation), and 'که آورد پیش' = 'that he brought forward/brought before (others)' where 'آورد' is the verb 'to bring/produce' and 'پیش' means 'before/in the presence of'. The inner 'که' again introduces the subordinate clause describing what was brought forward.
segments.s013-g8: The final clause 'که را بود از آن برتران پایه بیش' asks 'to whom (was) among those superior ones a greater rank/standing': 'که را' functions as 'to whom' (object/topic marker 'را' emphasizes the person), 'بود' = 'was', 'از آن برتران' = 'of those superior ones' (comparative/group), and 'پایه بیش' = 'a greater/more elevated foundation or rank' — the word order is inversed for emphasis (predicate 'پایه بیش' follows the group phrase).
segments.s013-g25: همی is an emphatic/continuous adverb (archaic poetic use) meaning 'constantly' or 'always'. تافت is the simple past of تابیدن 'to shine' used poetically ('shone'); the phrase ز او (here split as ز + و) marks the source 'from him'. فر شاهنشهی is the noun phrase 'imperial glory' (فر modified by شاهنشهی). Word order places the verb تافت before the noun phrase that 'shone' and the source phrase.
segments.s013-g26: چو introduces a simile 'like/as'. ماه دو هفته is a reduced noun phrase 'the moon of two weeks' (i.e. a two‑week‑old/full moon) where هفته here means 'week' and specifies age; ز سرو سهی is a parallel image 'from (a) dark cypress' with سرو 'cypress' and سهی a poetic epithet meaning 'dark' or 'dusky'. The two images are compared to the shining فر.
segments.s013-g27: This is a coordinated list of nouns joined by و: دد 'foes/enemies' and دام 'snares/traps' and هر جانور 'every animal'. کش is a modifier attached to جانور (an agentive/relational element here, forming something like 'beast‑(killing/‑moving)' or indicating a class of animals) and بدید is the past of دیدن 'he saw'. Syntax: subject/objects are listed, then the verb بدید applies to the whole series.
segments.s013-g28: ز گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'from the world' (ز = 'from', گیتی = 'world'). به نزدیک او is another locative phrase 'to/near him' (به indicating direction/toward, نزدیک 'near', او 'him'). آرمید is the past of آرمیدن 'to rest/lie down'. Together they mean that (those creatures) came from the world and lay down near him.
segments.s013-g29: دوتا is a numeral 'two'. می‌شدندی is an archaic past/habitual form (3rd plural) of شدن 'to become / to be', here meaning 'there were' or 'two came to be' — used poetically to say 'two things sat/stood'. بر تخت او is the locative phrase 'upon his throne'. The verb agrees with the implied plural 'they'.
segments.s013-g30: از آن 'from that' (referring back to the previous idea) + بر شده is a compound participial sense 'been put/placed on' or 'have arisen on'. فره و بخت او is the coordinated noun phrase 'his farr (divine glory) and his bāxt (luck/fortune)'. In sum: from that source his divine glory and fortune came to be (on his throne).
segments.s013-g31: به رسم نماز is an adverbial phrase 'according to the rite/custom of prayer' (به = 'in/according to', رسم = 'rite/custom', نماز = 'prayer'). آمدندیش is an archaic fused verb meaning 'they came to him' (آمدند + -یش = 'to him'); پیش here means 'before / in the presence of'. The word order places the manner phrase first, then the verbal phrase indicating who came.
segments.s013-g32: و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. ز او 'from him' marks source. برگرفتند is the simple past of برگرفتن and here means 'they took up' or 'they adopted'. آیین خویش is 'their own ritual/custom' — آیین 'rite/ceremony' and خوی + ش form the reflexive/possessive element 'their own'. Together: they adopted their practices from him.
segments.s013-g41: The phrase is a negative existential: 'به گیتی' ('in the world') + the verbal form 'نبودش' ('there was not/it was not') with the enclitic -ش referring back to the subject. 'کسی' in negative contexts means 'anyone/no one' and is the logical subject of the negation; 'دشمنا' is the plural noun 'enemies'. Word order is typical classical Persian: locative phrase → negated copula → subject → predicate noun.
segments.s013-g42: 'مگر' here means 'except' (introducing the sole exception). 'بدکنش' is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'ill‑doer' or 'bad‑behaved' describing the exception. 'ریمن' is treated as a proper name and 'آهرمنا' is the name/title Ahriman (the Evil One) following it; this is a classical appositive naming the exceptional enemy.
segments.s013-g43: 'به رشک' is an ablative/causal phrase meaning 'out of/through envy' (here 'به' functions like 'by/with' or 'through'). 'اندر' is the older/pre‑classical preposition 'in/within' used here to place the feeling inside Ahriman. 'آهرمن بدسگال' is a noun phrase: Ahriman described by the adjective 'بدسگال' ('ill‑omened/ill‑fated').
segments.s013-g44: 'همی' is the classical narrative prefix marking continuous or iterative action ('kept on / was continually'). 'رای زد' is an idiomatic combination: 'رای' = 'plan, counsel' and 'زد' (past of زدن) here = 'to set/strike/put (a plan)' — classical Persian often uses زدن with abstract objects. 'تا' introduces purpose/result ('so that / until'), followed by 'ببالید' (aorist/subjunctive meaning 'be proud / become proud') and the repeated noun 'بال' ('wing'), a poetic expression literally 'so that the wing might be proud' (i.e., so that it/one might grow proud).
segments.s013-g45: 'یکی' here means 'a certain' (introducing an indefinite figure). 'بچه' = 'child/offspring'. 'بودش' is past 'was' with enclitic -ش pointing back to the subject ('there was'). 'چو' = 'as/like', introducing a simile, and 'گرگ سترگ' is the comparison 'a wolf ...' where the adjective/epithet follows the noun (classical order).
segments.s013-g46: 'دلاور شده' is a perfect/participial phrase 'having become brave' (شده = 'become'). 'با سپاه بزرگ' is an instrumental/associative prepositional phrase 'with a great army' where 'با' = 'with' and 'سپاه بزرگ' = 'a large host/army' (adjective follows the noun).
segments.s013-g47: 'جهان شد' uses جهان ('the world') with شد ('became' / 'came to be'); in this construction 'شد بر' indicates a change of state or coming under the power of someone. 'بر آن دیو بچه سیاه' places the object after the preposition 'بر' ('upon/over/under') with the demonstrative 'آن' ('that') and the noun phrase 'دیو بچه سیاه' = 'that black demon‑child' (noun + modifiers follow the head).
segments.s013-g48: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'. 'بخت سیامک' = 'the fate/fortune of Siyamak' (بخت here = destiny). 'و زان پایگاه' continues the causal source: 'and from that stronghold' (زان = 'from that, from it'; پایگاه = 'base/stronghold'), indicating origins or causes for the world's coming under the demon‑child.
segments.s013-g73: Subject (سیامک) is followed by a prepositional-agent phrase 'به دست ...' meaning 'by the hand/of the agency of'. The adjective خروزان precedes the noun دیو as a standard modifier (adjective + noun = 'blazing demon').
segments.s013-g74: تبه گشت is a two-word verbal phrase: تبه (ruin/evil) + گشت (past of گشتن, 'became/was') = 'was ruined / came to ruin'. Then و ماند is a simple past sequence 'and remained/was left'. انجمن is the noun that follows ماند (the entity left behind). بی‌خدیو is marked in the source as an obscure/unclear term and is treated here as an unanalyzed adjective or complement.
segments.s013-g75: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. آگه (aware/informed) + شد (past of شدن) forms the verbal phrase 'became aware / learned'. از introduces the source/cause 'of the death of', with مرگ فرزند شاه in genitive order ('the death of the king's son').
segments.s013-g76: ز here means 'because of / from' and introduces the cause phrase. تیمار گیتی is a noun phrase 'the world's care/solace' with گیتی (world) modifying تیمار. بر او شد is a construction where شد ('became') takes سیاه ('dark/gloomy') as its predicate and بر او ('upon him') indicates the experiencer — literally 'the world's care became dark upon him.'
segments.s013-g77: فرود آمد is a verbal phrase 'descended / came down' (past). از تخت gives the source 'from the throne'. ویله کنان is an adverbial/participle phrase: ویله is obscure here but the -کنان ending is the poetic present-participial/adverbial form meaning 'while wailing/doing', so the whole unit qualifies the manner of descent.
segments.s013-g78: زنان is the agent/topic 'the women'. بر introduces what they act upon ('on/at' their heads, hair and faces). The sequence 'سر و موی و رخ را' lists the affected body parts; را is the definite/object marker attached to رخ but scopes over the coordinated list. کنان (the same participial -کنان) shows these actions happening continuously: 'tearing/striking at ...'.
segments.s013-g79: دو رخساره is a dual/collective noun phrase 'two cheeks / both faces'. پر خون is an adjectival phrase 'full of blood' (پر + noun = 'full of'). The conjunction و links to دل سوگوار, where دل 'heart' is described by the adjective سوگوار 'mourning/grieved'.
segments.s013-g80: دو دیده 'both eyes' followed by پر از نم 'full of moisture/tears' (پر + از + نم). چو is the simile marker 'like/as', and 'ابر بهار' ('spring cloud') is the image of the comparison; word order is noun + modifier (ابر modified by بهار).
segments.s013-g57: بگفتش ورا = ب + گفت + ش: a classical/cliticized past tense 'said to him'. The verb precedes the indirect object-clitic (ـش) and the separate pronoun ورا repeats/emphasizes 'to him'. زین سخن در به در is a short phrase following the verb indicating cause/state (literally 'from these words, door-to-door'); در به در is an idiom meaning distraught/wandering. Word order: verb + (clitic object) + (full object/pronominal) + comment phrase.
segments.s013-g58: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/which' or here an indirect question). چه سازد همی = 'what will (he) do' with سازد (present/future sense in classical style) and همی functioning as an older continuous/emphatic particle. با پدر = 'to/against his father' (با = with/against in Persian martial contexts). Overall this clause states the content of the speaker's worry.
segments.s013-g59: چون ... رسید = 'when/as ... reached'. In older Persian چـون can introduce a temporal clause ('when'); here 'سخن' (the report/words) is the subject and رسید is the past verb 'reached'. The phrase 'به گوش سیامک رسید' is a straightforward 'reached Siyâmak's ear/attention'.
segments.s013-g60: ز کردار بدخواه دیو پلید = a prepositional/attributive phrase giving the content of 'the words' (ز = 'from/about' in poetic usage). کردار 'deed' modified by the compound adjective بدخواه دیو 'the demon's ill-wishing' and پلید 'vile' further describes the demon. Word order: preposition + noun (کردار) + modifier chain (بدخواه دیو پلید).
segments.s013-g61: دل شاه بچه بر آمد به جوش = a poetic phrasing: 'دلِ شاهِ بچه' (the heart of the young king) is the subject; برآمد به جوش is a verbal phrase meaning 'rose in a boiling/surge' (i.e., was stirred). بر آمد is a compound verb (بر as separable/prepositional element + آمد 'came/rose'). به جوش is a complement indicating the stirred state ('into passion' or 'into a boil').
segments.s013-g62: سپاه انجمن کرد = 'he caused/held an assembly of the army' (کرد is the light verb 'did/caused'); the sequence means he called together troops. و بگشاد گوش = 'and opened his ear' i.e. 'listened attentively'; بگشاد here is a past/preterite form with the prefix بـ (classical stylistic auxiliary), followed by گوش as the object of listening.
segments.s013-g63: بپوشید تن را به چرم پلنگ = compound verb بپوشید (بـ + پوشید) 'he put on/wore'; تن را is the definite object (را marking); به چرم پلنگ indicates the material/garment 'in leopard leather/skin'. Word order: verb + object + prepositional phrase denoting what he wore.
segments.s013-g64: که جوشن نبود و نه آیین جنگ = a causal/explanatory clause introduced by که ('for/that'): جوشن نبود 'there was no cuirass/armor' (نبود = past 'was not'); و نه آیین جنگ = 'nor the customary practice/gear of war' (نه ... نه omitted first 'نه' implied before جوشن, but here 'و نه' links the negative second item). The clause explains why he wore the leopard hide.
segments.s013-g81: خروشی (a nominalized auditory noun) is the subject: 'a cry/clamor'. بر آمد is the past tense verb compound ('بر' + آمد) meaning 'arose' or 'came up'; in classical/poetic Persian the verbal prefix بر is written separated. ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'from the host/army' marking the source of the sound. به زار is an adverbial phrase where به + adjective زار (= 'lamenting, wailing') describes the manner ('in lament').
segments.s013-g82: کشیدند is past tense, 3rd-person-plural ('they drew/laid out'); here it takes the sense 'drew (a line)/formed'. صف is the direct object 'a line/formation' (battle line). بر در is a locative phrase ('on/upon the door/at the threshold') formed with the preposition بر plus در='door,gateway'; in poetry the possessor can follow, so شهریار names whose gate it is ('the king'). The whole phrase means 'they formed their ranks at the king's gate.'
segments.s013-g83: همه جامه‌ها is the nominal subject 'all (their) garments' or 'everyone's garments.' کرده is a perfect/participial form meaning 'made' or 'having made' and here functions as a predicative element ('had made / were made'). پیروزه رنگ is a compound where پیروزه specifies the color (turquoise) and رنگ means 'color' — together 'turquoise-colored.' The order (subject + کرده + color phrase) is typical in poetic Persian for 'had become/made X.'
segments.s013-g84: دو چشم is 'the two eyes' (a fixed phrase referring to the eyes). ابر here functions as a metaphorical modifier 'cloud-like' (a poetic image applied to the eyes), followed by the adjective خونین 'blood-red/bloodstained' as a postpositive adjective. و links that image to رخ بادرنگ: رخ = 'face' and بادرنگ is an adjectival compound 'multi-/variegated in color.' The coordination describes their appearance: 'eyes like bloody clouds and faces variegated.'
segments.s013-g85: دد و مرغ و نخچیر is a coordinated list 'beasts (or hostile creatures) and birds and game/prey.' گشته is the perfect/participle of شدن used here as 'having become' or 'having gathered,' and گروه is the noun 'group/troop' functioning as the complement: together 'having become a group' or 'grouped.' The phrase describes these creatures being gathered together.
segments.s013-g86: برفتند is past plural 'they departed/they went away.' ویله کنان is an adverbial participial phrase: ویله (an obscure/poetic term here) plus the present-participial ending کنان (literally 'doing so') gives the manner 'wailing/crying' or similar; such -کنان forms indicate the manner of the action. سوی کوه is the directional phrase 'toward the mountain.'
segments.s013-g87: برفتند (they departed) is the main past-tense verb. با introduces a manner/instrument phrase 'with.' سوگواری و درد are coordinated nouns 'mourning and grief/sorrow' describing how they departed. Together this segment reads 'they departed with mourning and sorrow.'
segments.s013-g88: ز درگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the court/threshold' (ز = 'from', درگاه here = 'royal court' or 'royal threshold'). کی شاه is a compound title/name (the epic title 'Kay/Shah' — often transliterated 'Kai-Shah'); grammatically it functions as the subject that follows the locative phrase in poetic order. برخاست is past singular 'rose/stood up.' گرد is an adverbial/particle ('around/about' or a classical emphatic/adverbial element) appended after the verb; here it does not change the core meaning 'he rose' but is a stylistic/poetic particle.
segments.s013-g49: سپه کرد is a short verbal clause: سپه (army, host) is the object/topic and کرد is the past tense of کردن 'to do/muster' — together 'he mustered an army'. و links to the following clause. نزدیک او راه جست is a movement clause: نزدیک (near) + او (him) sets the direction/target, and راه جستن (راه جست here, past) is a poetic verb meaning 'set out/advanced' (literally 'took the road' or 'sprang toward'). The verb is final as in Persian.
segments.s013-g50: همی is a classical/adverbial particle indicating continuity or immediacy ('then/meanwhile'). تخت و دیهیم is a conjoined nominal phrase 'the throne and the rural/peasant folk' that acts as the topical/frame element for what follows. کی is the temporal/subordinating adverb 'when', and شاه جست is a compact past clause 'the king leapt/rose' with جست (past of جستن) used poetically for a sudden movement; Persian places the verb at the end.
segments.s013-g51: همی here is an iterative/progressive particle ('he would/repeatedly'). گفت is the past verb 'he said' (in epic style). با هر کسی is a prepositional phrase 'to/with every person' (با + هر + کسی). رای خویش is the object 'his own counsel/opinion' — خویش is composed of خوی + the possessive enclitic ش 'his', so the phrase means 'his counsel'.
segments.s013-g52: جهان کرد is a transitive phrase 'he made/turned the world' (جهان = world as direct object before the verb, a common poetic order). یک‌سر پر آوای خویش is a predicative/accusative phrase 'wholly/full of his own voice': یک‌سر 'entirely', پر 'full (of)' governs the genitive phrase آوای خویش ('the voice of his own' with خویش = خوی + ش = 'his own'). The whole means he filled the world entirely with his own voice.
segments.s013-g53: کیومرث is the proper name subject. زین خود is a prepositional/ablative-like phrase 'from this saddle/seat of his' (زین = saddle, خود = himself/his own). کی آگاه بود is a rhetorical interrogative sequence: کی ('when' / 'ever') + آگاه بود (past of 'to be aware') — 'when/ever was he aware'. The verb is final (بود).
segments.s013-g54: که introduces the subordinate content clause 'that...'. تخت مهی را is a definite object/topic 'the moonlike/resplendent throne' (مهی modifies تخت); را is the definite object marker. جز او is the exclusory phrase 'except him' and شاه بود is the predication 'there was a king' (بود past 'was'), with verb-final order. Together the clause states the surprising fact that, aside from him, a king existed for that throne.
segments.s013-g55: یکایک is an adverb meaning 'one by one' / 'all at once' used here as a sudden/intensive adverb. بیامد is the simple past 'came'. خجسته is an epithet 'auspicious/blessed' modifying the proper name سروش (Soroush, the divine messenger/angel). Word order is adverb + verb + epithet + name.
segments.s013-g56: به سان is a classical simile marker 'like/as'. پری is the comparison term 'a fairy' (a beautiful, supernatural being). پلنگینه پوش is a compound: پلنگینه 'leopard-skin (cloak)' + پوش '‑wearer/‑clad', so together 'leopard-skin-clad' (i.e. wearing a leopard-skin robe). The whole phrase describes appearance by simile.
segments.s013-g65: پذیره شدش is a poetic/passive-like phrasing: پذیره (receive/accept) + شدش (شد + enclitic -ش = 'it happened to him' / 'became his') — together here meaning ‘received / took’ (with the resultive nuance). دیو را is the definite object (را marks the object), and جنگجوی appears after the verb phrase as the agent/subject (‘the warrior’). The order (verb + object + subject) is an inversion typical of epic Persian.
segments.s013-g66: سپه را (the host/army marked by را) is set up as the topic/scene. چو means 'when', and روی اندر آمد به روی is a formulaic expression: literally 'a face came into (اندر آمد) toward a face (به روی)' — i.e. 'they came face to face'. Note the repeated روی: the first refers to one side, the second to the other; اندر آمد + به expresses 'met / came up against'.
segments.s013-g67: سیامک is the subject (proper name) and بیامد is the simple past 'came'. برهنه تنا is an attributive phrase 'with his body naked' (برهنه = 'naked' modifying تنا = 'body'); word order places the adjective before the noun for poetic effect but yields the reading 'he came with his body bare.'
segments.s013-g68: بر آویخت is a compound: بر + آویخت (to hang/attach) = 'hung upon' or 'was hung on'. با introduces the agent/instrument (here 'by/with'), پور is 'son' and آهرمنا is the proper name (Ahrimna / Ahriman). In context this reads: 'he was hung on by Ahrimna's son' (با marking the doer/agent).
segments.s013-g69: بزد is the simple past 'he struck/smote'. چنگ (here 'claw') is the instrument/object and وارونه ('upturned, inverted') modifies it. دیو سیاه ('the black demon') names the agent and appears after the verb phrase — again a poetic inversion where subject follows verb+object.
segments.s013-g70: دوتا ('two') is the numeral; اندر آورد is a compound verb 'brought in / brought' (اندر here part of the verbal phrase), and بالای شاه means 'onto/above the king' (بالای = 'upon/above', شاه = 'king'). Together: 'he brought two onto the king' (literal; the precise referent of 'two' depends on context).
segments.s013-g71: فکند is the past tense 'threw/cast'. آن تن شاهزاده is a noun phrase 'that body of the prince' (آن = 'that' modifying تن; شاهزاده in apposition). به خاک is the prepositional phrase 'to the earth / onto the ground'. The verb governs the following locative phrase.
segments.s013-g72: به چنگال is instrumental 'by/with the claw' (به used here to mark instrument), کردش is کرد + enclitic ش ('he made/did to him' — the clitic = 'him'), and کمرگاه چاک is a short clause meaning 'the waist (کمرگاه) gash/cleft' (چاک = 'gash'). Put together: 'with its claw he made his waist a gash' = 'he tore open his waist with the claw.'
segments.s013-g97: This is a temporal/clausal opener. کی here functions as the interrogative/adverb 'when' (classical usage) and is followed by the noun phrase 'نامور سر سوی آسمان' (famed one + head + toward heaven). Word order is Persian (modifier before head): نامور (adjective) modifies the implied subject, سر is the literal 'head' that is being raised, and سوی + آسمان marks the direction 'toward the heavens'.
segments.s013-g98: This continues the clause with the verbal action. سر بر آورد is a fixed literary collocation meaning 'raised (his) head' (بر here as a prefix/particle + آورد 'bring/raise'); آوَرد is third-person singular past. The conjunction و links the next predication; بدخواست is an adjective 'ill-omened/inauspicious' describing the look or portent, and بر بدگمان uses بر = 'toward/against' to mark the target (بدگمان 'the mistrustful/suspicious [one]').
segments.s013-g99: This is an object phrase placed before the verb, typical in Persian narrative: بر آن برترین نام یزدانش را = 'upon that supreme name of his God'. بر functions as 'on/upon' here; برترین is the superlative modifying نام; یزدانش is 'his God' (possessive -ش) and را is the definite-object marker attaching to the whole noun phrase.
segments.s013-g100: Two coordinated verbs follow. بخواند is a third-person singular narrative/past-form (classical/poetic form with the b- prefix used in narration) meaning 'he called/invoked' (here 'call upon' the divine name). The conjunction و links to بپالود, another past third-person verb meaning 'he cleansed' or 'he wiped/cleansed'. مژگانش را ('his eyelashes' + object marker) is the object of بپالود.
segments.s013-g101: زان پس is a classical compound meaning 'after that' (زان = from that, پس = after/then). The phrase به کین سیامک uses به + کین ('to/for vengeance') with سیامک (proper name) to indicate the aim/purpose 'to avenge Siyamak.' شتافت is the past third-person of شتافتن 'to hasten/rush', so the clause reads 'he hastened to take vengeance for/over Siyamak.'
segments.s013-g102: This is a short result clause: 'شب و روز' (night and day) followed by آرام (rest/peace). خفتن is the infinitive 'to sleep' and نیافت is the negative past/past perfective of یافتن with the classical negative prefix نی- meaning 'did not find/obtain'. Together: 'day and night he did not find rest, nor could he sleep.' The verb نیافت is third-person past, applying to both آرام (rest) and خفتن (sleep).
segments.s013-g89: نشستند is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person plural verbal form meaning “they sat / remained seated” (here idiomatically “they sat in mourning”). سالی is an adverbial/temporal phrase “for a year” placed after the verb. چنین modifies the following adjective سوگوار and means “thus/so,” so the whole phrase says they remained “so mournful / in such mourning.”
segments.s013-g90: پیام آمد is a simple clause with پیـام as the subject and آمد (past) as the verb: “a message came.” از introduces a source (prepositional phrase) and is followed by the title داور modified by کردگار (apposition/epithet) to mean the divine judge — “from the Judge, the Creator.” Word order here is subject–verb plus a following from‑phrase indicating source.
segments.s013-g91: درود آوریدش is an imperative construction: درود (greeting/blessing) plus the verb آورید (2nd‑person plural imperative ‘bring/offer’) with the enclitic object ش = “him/it” (آورید-ش). The noun phrase خجسته سروش is a vocative/epithet following the imperative — “O blessed/auspicious Soroush,” where خجسته modifies سروش.
segments.s013-g92: کز این بیش is a temporal/adverbial cluster: کز (from/whence) + این (this) + بیش (more/any longer) = “from this time on / henceforth.” مخروش is an adjectival/verbal admonition meaning “(be) tumultuous/roaring” (here understood as an admonition ‘do not be tumultuous’ in context). و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” باز آر هوش is a small clause where باز “back/again” modifies آر — an archaic or poetic imperative form of ‘bring/restore’ (short form of آوردن) — with هوش “intellect/sense” as its object: “and restore (the) senses.”
segments.s013-g93: سپه ساز و برکش are two coordinated imperatives. سپه ساز functions as an order to “make/prepare the army” (ساز here acts as an imperative verb ‘make/array’ in poetic register). و links the second imperative برکش, an archaic/poetic imperative ‘raise/lift up’ (from برکشیدن). به فرمان من is an agentive/instrumental phrase “by my command / at my order,” with من as the speaker’s first‑person pronoun.
segments.s013-g94: بر آور is a split verbal form (prefix بر + verb آور ‘bring’) functioning as an imperative “bring forth.” یکی گرد is an idiomatic, compact noun phrase meaning “one band/contingent” (یکی = one/a certain; گرد here is a poetic word for a troop/group). از آن انجمن is a prepositional phrase “from that assembly,” specifying the source of the contingent.
segments.s013-g95: از آن is a deictic prepositional phrase “from that.” بد کنش is a compound adjective+noun meaning “bad‑doing / evil‑acting” and modifies دیو (demon), so بد کنش دیو = “the demon of evil deeds” or “that evil‑doing demon.” روی زمین is a locative phrase “on the face/surface of the earth,” with روی used as the prepositional/surface sense “upon/on.”
segments.s013-g96: بپرداز is an imperative (from پرداختن/بپرداختن) meaning “attend to / deal with / cast down” in the sense of acting against the foe. و = “and.” پردخته کن combines the participial/adjectival form پردخته (literally “made/processed/wrought”) with the imperative verb کن “make/do,” so the sequence means “make (it) made/wrought” idiomatically “make/turn (someone) into [something]

sentences.s013-s5: The scholar of the ancient record who recounts the tales of the heroes.
sentences.s013-s6: Thus it is said that Kayōmarth brought the ceremony (the rite) of throne and crown, and he was king.
sentences.s013-s7: When the sun came into the sign of Aries, the world became endowed with (royal) glory, rites, and water.
sentences.s013-s8: It shone thus from the sign of the Ram, so that the world became altogether young from that.
sentences.s013-s9: Kayomarth became the headman (ruler) of the world; first he made a dwelling inside a mountain.
sentences.s013-s10: His fortune and his throne rose up to the mountain; he donned a leopard‑skin cloak together with his company.
sentences.s013-s11: From him there constantly came provision/care, so that his clothing was new and his food was new.
sentences.s013-s12: He reigned as king in the world for thirty years; in goodness he was like the sun at its proper hour.
sentences.s013-s17: A son was given to him — a fair‑faced, skillful one, and, like his father, one who sought renown.
sentences.s013-s18: His name was Siâmak and he was auspicious; Keyumars's heart was alive for him.
sentences.s013-s19: He wept for him from (deep) love to his very soul; from the fear of their separation he was consumed.
sentences.s013-s20: One day a time came upon this affair; the king's fortune became radiant.
sentences.s013-s1: What does the peasant's speaker say first — he who sought a great name in the world?
sentences.s013-s2: Who was that one who placed the diheîm upon his head? No one in those ages keeps it in memory.
sentences.s013-s3: Except that the son remembers from his father; he will tell you, one by one, from door to door.
sentences.s013-s4: Whose great name he brought forward — who among those superior ones had a loftier standing?
sentences.s013-s13: His imperial glory continually shone from him like a full moon and like a dark cypress.
sentences.s013-s14: He saw foes and snares and every kind of beast; from the world they came and lay down near him.
sentences.s013-s15: Two were (upon) his throne; from that were made his farr and his fortune.
sentences.s013-s16: They came before him in the manner of prayer, and from him they adopted their own custom.
sentences.s013-s21: There was no enemy in the world except that evil‑doer Rīman Ahrimna.
sentences.s013-s22: Out of envy, the ill‑omened Ahriman took counsel and plotted until the wing grew proud.
sentences.s013-s23: There was a certain child, like a great‑eyed wolf; he became a valiant one with a large army.
sentences.s013-s24: The world came under that black demon‑child, by the fate of Siyamak and from that stronghold.
sentences.s013-s37: Siyamak fell by the hand of the blazing demon and the assembly was left bereft (the final word is obscure).
sentences.s013-s38: When the king learned of his son's death, the world's solace turned dark for him.
sentences.s013-s39: He descended from the throne, wailing, and the women tore at their heads, hair, and faces.
sentences.s013-s40: Both cheeks were stained with blood and the heart was mourning; both eyes were full of tears like spring clouds.
sentences.s013-s29: He told him this, and from these words he was driven door to door, wondering what the enemy would do to his father.
sentences.s013-s30: When the words reached Siyâmak's ear concerning the evil deed of the vile demon.
sentences.s013-s31: The young king's heart was stirred; he summoned the troops and listened attentively.
sentences.s013-s32: He clad his body in a leopard's hide, for there was no cuirass nor the customary war-gear.
sentences.s013-s41: A cry arose from the host in lamentation; they drew up their ranks at the king's gate.
sentences.s013-s42: Everyone had their garments made turquoise in color; two eyes were cloud-blooded and the faces variegated.
sentences.s013-s43: Beasts and birds and game, having formed groups, went off — wailing — toward the mountain.
sentences.s013-s44: They departed with mourning and grief; from the royal court the Kai-Shah rose.
sentences.s013-s25: He mustered an army and drew near him; meanwhile the throne and the rural folk — when the king leapt.
sentences.s013-s26: He was saying to everyone his own counsel, and he made the world entirely full of his own voice.
sentences.s013-s27: When was Kayumarth himself aware from that (saddle) that, except him, there was a king on the resplendent throne?
sentences.s013-s28: Suddenly (one by one) the auspicious Soroush came, like a fairy clad in a leopard-skin robe.
sentences.s013-s33: The army's warrior confronted the demon when they came face to face.
sentences.s013-s34: Siyamak came, his body naked; he was hung on (seized) by Ahrimna's son.
sentences.s013-s35: The black demon struck with an upturned claw and brought two onto the king.
sentences.s013-s36: He cast that body of the prince to the earth; with his claw he made the prince's waist gape.
sentences.s013-s49: When the famed one lifted his head toward the heavens and, ill-omened, cast a look at the mistrustful man.
sentences.s013-s50: He invoked that supreme name of his God, and he cleansed (wiped) his eyelashes.
sentences.s013-s51: And after that he hurried to avenge Siyamak; day and night he found no rest nor could he sleep.
sentences.s013-s45: They sat in mourning like that for a year; a message came from the divine Judge.
sentences.s013-s46: Offer him greetings, O auspicious Soroush (heavenly messenger); henceforth do not be tumultuous, and restore (their) senses.
sentences.s013-s47: Marshal the army and lift it up at my command; bring forth one band from that assembly.
sentences.s013-s48: Drive that ill‑doing, demon‑faced one from the face of the earth, and strike him down; and cleanse the heart of hatred.
